title: Statistical Power & The Tides
text:
One of the hardest concepts for psychology students to grasp is Statistical Power.
We teach them definitions like “1 - beta” or “the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false.” These definitions are accurate, but they are intuitive dead ends. They don’t help a student visualize why their experiment failed.
Instead, I prefer to teach Power using the metaphor of The Tides.

title: Statistical Power & The Tides
text:
The Rock and The Water
Imagine you are standing on a cliff looking down at a turbulent ocean. Your goal is simple: Spot the Rock.
In this metaphor: * The Rock is your Effect Size. It is the real phenomenon you are trying to find. * The Water is the Noise (measurement error, individual differences). * The Tide Level is your Statistical Power.

Scenario A: Low Power (High Tide)
Imagine the tide is incredibly high. The water is deep and choppy. Somewhere beneath those waves, there is a rock. It is definitely there (the effect exists!), but the noise of the water is completely covering it.
You look for an hour and see nothing. You conclude: “There is no rock here.” In statistics, we call this a Type II Error (False Negative). The rock didn’t vanish; you just lacked the power to see it through the noise.


Scenario B: High Power (Low Tide)
Now, imagine we lower the tide. The water level drops. The waves settle. Suddenly, the jagged tip of the rock breaks the surface. You didn’t make the rock bigger; you just removed the noise that was hiding it.
This is what happens when we increase sample size or reduce measurement error (e.g., using a precise VR tracker instead of a stopwatch). We lower the water level so the signal becomes visible.

title: Statistical Power & The Tides
text:
The “Big Rock” Fallacy
Many researchers try to fix a “High Tide” problem by looking for a “Bigger Rock.”
They think: “If I just make my manipulation stronger (a louder noise, a scarier VR cliff), the effect will be massive, and I’ll see it even if my sample is small.”
This works… sometimes. If the rock is a mountain, you will see it even at high tide. But most effects in psychology aren’t mountains; they are pebbles. We are looking for subtle changes in reaction time or slight shifts in emotion regulation.
If you are looking for a pebble at high tide, no amount of squinting (p-hacking) will save you. You need to drain the water.

title: Statistical Power & The Tides
text:
Why Simulation Matters
This is why I advocate for Simulation Science.
In a traditional field study, the “weather” is unpredictable. The waves are choppy (high noise). In a Virtual Reality lab, I control the weather. I can turn off the wind. I can perfectly standardize the environment.
By using VR, we are essentially building a breakwall around our study. We calm the waters, reducing the noise variance, which makes even small rocks visible.
We don’t need “bigger” effects; we need calmer water.

Enjoyed this metaphor? Subscribe to the newsletter for more weekly thoughts on methods and mechanics.

title: Consulting Services
text:
I offer specialized human factors consulting for labs, startups, and technology teams building systems that people must use reliably—in the real world, under real constraints.
I help you design and evaluate tools, interfaces, and workflows for usability, safety, and human performance. When needed, I build the technical research architecture to support strong inference: studies that are ecologically valid, reproducible, and decision-relevant.

title: Consulting Services
text:
What I Deliver

Clear evidence about what users can (and cannot) do with your system
Risk-reducing recommendations grounded in data (not opinions)
Validated prototypes and/or simulation-based evaluations before costly deployment
Reusable measurement & analysis pipelines your team can run again

title: Consulting Services
text:
My Services & Expertise

🧠 Human Factors & Usability Evaluation🥽 VR & Simulation for Human Performance📊 Measurement, Experimental Design & Data Analysis


Do you need to know whether your product, interface, or procedure will work for people in practice—and where errors, friction, or confusion are coming from?
I run human factors evaluations that map real user goals onto real task demands. This includes identifying failure points (where users misinterpret cues, choose the wrong action, or miss critical information), and turning those findings into concrete design changes.

Services

Task & workflow analysis: Decompose tasks, decision points, and handoffs; identify cognitive load and bottlenecks.
Usability testing: Plan and run studies; define success metrics; capture qualitative + behavioral measures.
Error analysis & risk-oriented evaluation: Identify likely error pathways and mitigation strategies (interface, procedure, training).
Human-centered requirements & design guidance: Translate findings into implementable requirements and prioritized recommendations.
Evaluation reporting for stakeholders: Concise, decision-ready outputs for product, engineering, and leadership.





Do you want to test an interface, workflow, or training approach in conditions that are closer to real life—before you roll it out?
As a cognitive experimental psychologist, I have harnessed VR simulations to create high-control, high-realism testbeds—ideal for investigating human factors questions where field testing would be expensive or dangerous.

Services

Simulation-based prototyping: Build VR scenarios that reflect real-world tasks, constraints, and environmental demands.
Human factors evaluation in VR: Measure human performance, errors, attention, workload, and adaptation across conditions.
Technology & sensor integration: Synchronise events in VR with data streams (e.g., ECG/EEG/EDA, eye-tracking, motion capture).
Presence & experiential realism (when it matters): Audit and optimise factors that influence user engagement, behavior, and transfer.
Bespoke VR development: From simple environments to interactive simulations tailored to your evaluation goals.





Do you have complex data but need trustworthy answers—or do you need a study designed so the results actually generalize?
I build reproducible analytic pipelines and design studies that support clear conclusions. This is especially useful when your data are messy, multi-modal, or high-frequency (e.g., VR telemetry, biosignal feedback).

Services

Study design for strong inference: Hypotheses, operational definitions, sampling, power/sensitivity, and analysis plans.
Statistical modeling: Mixed models, GAMs, Bayesian hierarchical models, and (when appropriate) machine learning—aligned to the question.
Physiological signal processing: Cleaning, artifact handling, feature extraction, and interpretation for physiological (e.g., ECG/EDA/EEG) data streams.
Reproducible reporting: Automated, transparent workflows that produce publication- and stakeholder-ready reports.
Visualization & dashboards: Aesthetically pleasant publication-ready figures and interactive dashboards that clearly portray the story of your findings.

title: Consulting Services
text:
Engagements That Fit Well

Product teams shipping systems where usability failures are costly (health, safety, public services, enterprise tools)
R&D groups needing credible evidence for claims, funding, or regulatory conversations
Training & operations teams testing whether learning transfers to real performance
Labs & startups building or validating VR/simulation-based research and assessment tools

If you’re unsure whether your project is “human factors” or “research,” that’s fine—many projects are both. We can frame the question in a way that produces actionable evidence.

title: Consulting Services
text:
How I Work
I use an open, reproducible model of consulting. You don’t just receive a report—you receive the assets that make the work durable: code, documentation, and (where relevant) prototypes and data dictionaries. The goal is to leave you with a capability, not a dependency.

1. The Discovery Call (Free)
We schedule a 30-minute call to understand your context, constraints, and what “success” looks like. I’ll tell you candidly whether I’m a good fit—and if not, what kind of specialist you likely need.
Outcome: A clear go/no-go decision and a rough path to scope.


2. First Deliverable: The Blueprint
I deliver a blueprint that defines scope, measures, and deliverables.

For human factors projects: task model, evaluation plan, success criteria, and reporting format

For VR projects: storyboard + “grey-box” prototype video

For data work: mock-up of the final report/dashboard + analysis plan

Outcome: You know exactly what you’re paying for, and what decisions the work will support.


3. Second Deliverable: Execution & Handoff
I build, evaluate, and deliver—then train your team to run and extend the system. You receive commented code and clear documentation (plus walkthrough videos when helpful).
Outcome: A sustainable toolchain and evidence you can reuse.

text:
Ready to start?
Book a Free Discovery Call

title: Research
text:
My research is driven by a single, overarching goal: to establish a rigorous science of human behavior in simulated environments.
For decades, psychology has faced a “validity trade-off.” We could study behavior in the real world (high ecological validity, low control) or in the lab (low ecological validity, high control). My work demonstrates that Virtual Reality (VR) allows us to break this trade-off, enabling us to study complex, high-stakes emotional experiences—like fear, awe, and disgust—without sacrificing millisecond-level experimental precision.

title: Research
text:
Core Research Areas

1. The Nature of Presence
What makes a virtual experience feel “real”?
My recent work challenges the traditional “bottom-up” view that physiological arousal drives the sense of presence. In our recent paper in Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, we demonstrated that subjective fear—not physiological arousal—shapes the experience of presence.
Using a high-fidelity “Virtual Plank” simulation, we dissociated these two systems. This supports constructionist theories of emotion (e.g., Lisa Feldman Barrett), suggesting that “Presence” is a top-down cognitive conceptualization, not just a visceral reaction to sensory input.






Key Finding



“Presence is not just about higher resolution graphics; it is about the meaning the user assigns to the environment. If the brain constructs a threat narrative, the environment becomes ‘real,’ regardless of heart rate.”




2. Emotion Regulation Under Threat
How do we regulate fear when the threat feels immediate?
Traditional labs study fear using static images on a computer screen. But looking at a picture of a spider is fundamentally different from standing on a ledge 80 floors up.
I use VR to induce high-intensity threat states in the lab. My “Virtual Plank” and “Filthy Bathroom” (Disgust) paradigms allow us to test: * Inhibitory Control: Does fear paralyze us or speed us up? (See my work on the Stop Signal Task on a plank). * Reappraisal vs. Suppression: Can you “think” your way out of fear when your visual system is screaming “danger”? * Physiological Synchrony: How do EEG and EDA signals align with behavioral freezing?


3. Methodological Innovation
Standardizing the “VR Neuroscience Lab”
A major barrier to adoption is the technical complexity of setting up a VR lab. My paper “Launching Your VR Neuroscience Laboratory” serves as a blueprint for other researchers. I am committed to Open Science: * Open Assets: I release the Unity environments for my tasks so others can replicate them exactly. * Open Code: Data analysis pipelines (R/Python) are shared to ensure reproducibility.

title: Research
section: Future Vision: The Simulation Science Lab
text:
Future Vision: The Simulation Science Lab
My long-term aim is to establish a dedicated Simulation Science Laboratory. This facility will move beyond simple HMD setups to fully integrated multi-modal simulation, combining:

Wireless EEG & Mobile fNIRS for neural imaging in motion.
Full-body Motion Capture for behavioral kinematics.
Haptic Feedback to “close the loop” of sensory immersion.

This lab will not just study VR; it will use VR as a tool to understand the fundamental mechanisms of the predictive brain.

title: Research
text:
Selected Paradigms


🏔️ The Virtual Plank
Status: Published (JEP: General) A high-fidelity height exposure task. Participants walk out onto a narrow plank extending from a skyscraper. * Measures: Subjective Units of Distress (SUDS), Heart Rate Variability (HRV), Postural Sway.


🤢 The Disgust Simulation
Status: In Prep A “Filthy Bathroom” VR environment designed to trigger core disgust. * Goal: To test if disgust impacts moral decision-making and motor inhibition differently than fear.


🚀 The “Awe” Dome
Status: Ongoing (Marsden Grant) A spaceship-style dome overlooking a nebula. Designed to induce “Awe”—the feeling of vastness and accommodation. * Collaboration: Working with Gina Grimshaw to map the physiology of positive awe states.


🧠 EEG-VR Integration
Status: Methodological Developing pipelines to synchronize Unity event markers with BrainVision EEG amplifiers to correct for timing latency.

Cognitive Psychologist | Experimentalist | Technologist
I am a Cognitive Experimental Psychologist from Te Whanganui-a-Tara, Aotearoa | Wellington, New Zealand.
I conduct research at the intersection of cognition and emotion. The experiments I design investigate such questions as: “Is our cognitive control (e.g., inhibition, attentional control, planning) enhanced or hindered when we experience fear?” and “Can a mind construct human-like concepts of emotions if that mind has no physical body?”
human factors and ergonomics, ecologically valid experimentation, VR and simulation science, and the emerging challenge of human-AI hybrid workflow design. I use tools like virtual reality, mobile biosensors, and data analytics to deliver evidence that holds up under scrutiny—not opinions.
Whether you need a usability evaluation, a controlled experiment, a VR-based training prototype, or a strategy for integrating agentic AI into your team’s workflow without losing what makes human judgement irreplaceable—I provide end-to-end consulting services: from problem framing and study design to data collection, analysis, and clear recommendations your team can implement.
Hire Me for Consulting View Publications  Download Academic CV

title: Christopher N. Maymon, PhD
text:
How I Can Help


🧠 Human Factors & Usability Evaluation
Are users struggling, making errors, or developing workarounds? I evaluate human–system interaction to identify where interfaces, procedures, and environments create friction—then I deliver actionable fixes.
Typical work includes task analysis, workflow mapping, usability testing, cognitive walkthroughs, error analysis, and evidence-based recommendations for safer and more intuitive systems.


🥽 VR & Simulation for Human Performance
I build and use high-fidelity VR simulations that let teams test designs and undergo training in realistic conditions—before products are deployed at scale.
I support organisations that want to (a) establish VR capability, (b) prototype and evaluate procedures or interfaces, or (c) develop bespoke simulations for human factors research and performance training.


📊 Experimental Design, Measurement & Data Analysis
If you need answers that hold up under scrutiny, I design studies that are reproducible, interpretable, and aligned to operational decisions. I build measurement plans, choose appropriate statistical models, and communicate results clearly to technical and non-technical stakeholders.
This is ideal for A/B tests, field trials, intervention evaluations, and any situation where you need credible evidence—not just opinions.


🤖 Human-AI Hybrid Workflow Design
AI is transforming how teams work—but the hardest problem isn’t the technology, it’s designing workflows where agentic AI systems and human judgement genuinely complement each other.
I help laboratories and companies architect hybrid workflows that leverage the speed, scalability, and consistency of AI agents while preserving the essential role of human intuition, creativity, and ethical reasoning at every level of distributed teamwork. The goal is augmentation with dignity—not replacement.

title: About Me
text:
My journey into Virtual Reality (VR) began with a frustration that every experimental psychologist eventually faces: The trade-off between experimental control and ecological validity.
During my PhD at Victoria University of Wellington, I was investigating Theory of Mind—how we understand the intentions of others. Like many before me, I found myself stuck between two imperfect choices. I could run highly controlled experiments on a computer screen (which lacked verisimilitude: the messy reality of actual environments), or I could run field studies (which are more ecologically valid but are impossible to control scientifically).
Generally speaking This disconnect has seemed intractable. We were studying human behavior by removing the “human” and the “behavior” from the equation.



By complete chance, I stumbled upon a basic VR setup being tested in our department. I realized almost immediately that this technology wasn’t just a gimmick for gamers; it was the solution to the Trade-Off.
VR allowed me to place participants in “real” situations—standing at the edge of a cliff, walking a plank, or interacting with a virtual agent—while maintaining millisecond-level control over every photon of light they saw.
I pivoted my research focus entirely. I taught myself Unity and C#, not because I wanted to be a developer, but because I needed better tools to answer scientific questions. This work culminated in my research on Presence—understanding not just if people feel fear in VR, but why they treat virtual threats as real.



As I built more complex simulations, I realized another problem: Reproducibility.
A text-based methods section in a PDF cannot possibly capture the complexity of a VR environment. If I say “the participant stood on a ledge,” how high was it? How was the lighting? What was the physics engine doing?
This realization turned me into a fierce advocate for Open Science. I believe that code is the method. Today, I publish my datasets, analysis code (R/Python), and VR environments (Unity) openly. I advocate for a science where “replicating a study” means downloading the environment and hitting ‘Play’.



Today, I wear three hats: 1. Lecturer: Teaching the next generation of psychologists how to use these tools. I teach courses on Experimental Design, VR Development, and Data Analysis. 2. Researcher: Investigating research questions about emotions, emotion regulations, the relationship between emotion and cognition, and how our minds construct our reality. 3. Consultant: Helping other labs and companies build valid, reliable, and rigorous VR experiments.
If you are interested in discussing experimental design, VR development, or data analysis, please get in touch.
